1.synthesis:
The opening of 1 Samuel deposits us immediately in the interior life of a barren woman. Elkanah of Ephraim has two wives — Peninnah, who bears children, and Hannah, whom the Lord has closed. The narrator specifies that Elkanah loved Hannah, yet this love cannot compensate for the vacancy she carries. Year after year the family makes pilgrimage to Shiloh to sacrifice before the Lord of hosts — a title that appears here for the first time in the Hebrew Bible, Yahweh Tseba'ot, the God of armies — and year after year Peninnah provokes Hannah to make her miserable. The Hebrew says she was her adversary, her rival, pressing upon the nerve of shame that childlessness represented in the ancient world. Hannah cannot eat; her husband's doubled portion for her is both expression of love and reminder of what she lacks. Elkanah's gentle but obtuse question — "Am I not better to you than ten sons?" — captures the blindness of even well-meaning comfort that does not enter the sufferer's actual grief.

John Gill observes that the precise genealogy Elkanah receives signals the narrator's intention to locate this story in respectable Levitical lineage that will culminate in Samuel's priestly role. Matthew Henry underscores that barrenness here is explicitly theological: "the Lord had shut up her womb" — not accident, not fault, but divine administration that will make Samuel's birth unmistakably miraculous. The repeated annual journey to Shiloh is significant: worship is the frame within which grief is carried rather than avoided. Hannah does not withdraw from the sanctuary because God has seemingly withheld blessing; she appears before him precisely in her need. This posture of persistent presence before a silent God is itself a form of faith. The pattern anticipates the lament psalms, where the worshiper brings the full weight of suffering into the sanctuary and addresses God directly rather than retreating into despair or denial.

Literary structure here is concentric: the narrator establishes family, then barrenness, then the annual feast that sharpens the contrast between Peninnah's fruitfulness and Hannah's emptiness, then Elkanah's insufficient comfort. Everything drives toward the sanctuary as the only court where Hannah's case can be heard.
1.historical_context: Shiloh was the central Israelite sanctuary during the period of the Judges, housing the ark of the covenant after its installation by Joshua (Josh 18:1). Annual pilgrimage festivals — Passover, Weeks, and Tabernacles — required Israelite households to appear before the Lord. The sacrifice Elkanah offers is likely a shelamim (peace offering), which involved sharing the meat among family members after the priestly portions were given. Polygamy, while practiced in ancient Israel, is consistently depicted in the biblical narrative as a source of domestic conflict; the rivalry between co-wives (also visible in the Sarah-Hagar and Rachel-Leah cycles) is a structural feature of these households. Barrenness in the ancient Near East carried social stigma: a woman's primary social contribution was reproduction, and childlessness was often interpreted as divine disfavor or moral failing. The title Yahweh Tseba'ot appears here without prior biblical occurrence in this form, possibly reflecting a distinctively Shilonite liturgical tradition associated with the ark, which was conceived as the Lord's throne or war-chariot amid the heavenly hosts.
1.literary_structure: The chapter opens with a genealogical anchor (1:1–2) establishing Elkanah's identity and Hannah's rival. The narrative then enters a repetitive structure — the phrase "year by year" (shanah be-shanah) recurs, giving the impression of cyclical suffering trapped in time. The feast scene (1:4–8) concentrates the tension: Elkanah distributes portions, Peninnah provokes, Hannah weeps and cannot eat, and Elkanah responds with a question that reveals his limits. This compression into a single feast scene is representative of a repeated pattern, making the pity acute. The move to the sanctuary in verse 9 will begin the turn; verses 1–8 are pure preparation — the weight that makes the prayer of verses 9–18 comprehensible.
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry draws from this opening a pastoral principle: God sometimes withholds temporal blessings from his most beloved people precisely to drive them deeper into prayer and dependence. Hannah's closed womb is not punishment but preparation; the instrument of her humiliation will become the occasion of Israel's salvation through Samuel. Gill notes that the phrase "the Lord had shut up her womb" (1:6) is deliberately divine-passive — sovereignty is asserted without explanation, and Hannah must live inside that sovereignty without initial clarity about its purpose. For contemporary application, this establishes that chronic, unchosen suffering — the kind that returns year after year with maddening regularity — is not outside God's government. The fact that Hannah continued annual pilgrimage to Shiloh even while in pain models the discipline of communal worship sustained through seasons when God feels absent or hostile. Elkanah's love, real as it is, cannot fill the God-shaped wound; only the Lord of hosts can open what he has closed.
1.christology: Hannah's suffering anticipates the pattern of election through affliction that runs from the patriarchs to the prophets and reaches its apex in the Messiah himself. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the Lord's sovereign closing and opening of wombs — Hannah's here, Sarah's in Gen 21:1, Rachel's in Gen 30:22 — establishes a divine habit of calling the decisive figures of redemptive history into existence through impossible births. Samuel, born of this closed womb, will anoint the line of David; David's line will bring the Son of David. The Virgin Birth is not therefore an isolated anomaly but the culmination of a pattern in which God repeatedly bypasses natural generation to signal that the child about to arrive is his sovereign gift, not human achievement. The title Yahweh Tseba'ot that Hannah will invoke in her prayer (1:11) is the God who commands the armies of heaven — the same Lord whose army the angel of the Lord leads in Josh 5:14, and whose glory fills the temple in Isa 6:3. The Child born to set his people free will be born into this tradition of impossible divine gift.

9.synthesis:
Hannah rises from the feast she cannot eat and goes to pray before the Lord at the doorpost of the Shiloh temple. The prayer is silent — her lips move but her voice is unheard — and it is accompanied by a vow: if the Lord of hosts will look on her affliction and give her a son, she will dedicate him to the Lord all the days of his life, and no razor shall touch his head. The Nazirite formula here is deliberate: Samuel will be consecrated from birth, a lifelong Nazirite like Samson before him, but unlike Samson wholly faithful to his calling. Eli the priest sees Hannah's mouth moving without sound and accuses her of drunkenness — a comic misjudgment that reveals Eli's spiritual obtuseness. Hannah corrects him with dignity: she is pouring out her soul before the Lord, not drunk on wine. Eli, corrected, offers a priestly blessing: "Go in peace, and the God of Israel grant your petition." Hannah's countenance changes; she eats, and her face is no longer sad.

Matthew Henry remarks on the theology of Hannah's prayer: she asks not for vindication against Peninnah, not for public honor, but for a son — and immediately frames that petition as an offering. She is asking God for something she intends to return to him. This is not transaction but transformation: the praying shapes the one who prays even before the answer arrives. John Gill notes that the word translated "affliction" (oni) is the same root used for Israel's slavery in Egypt — Hannah frames her barrenness in the most serious of terms, a condition requiring divine deliverance, not merely divine convenience. The silence of the prayer is significant: this is not public performance but private address, the soul speaking directly to God in its own language. Eli's mistake — priest misreading genuine prayer as sin — underlines that institutional religion can miss authentic encounter with God even when it is happening steps away.

The narrative arc bends at verse 18: "Her face was no longer sad." Peace arrives before the pregnancy — the change in Hannah precedes the biological answer by months. This is a crucial theological datum: the prayer itself has done something, has moved something in Hannah's relationship with God, independent of the material outcome. She has been heard, and she knows it.
9.historical_context: The Nazirite vow is specified in Num 6: separation from wine, no contact with the dead, and uncut hair for the duration of the vow. Hannah's vow extends the consecration to Samuel's entire life, making him a permanent Nazirite — a more radical dedication than the temporary vows typical in the law. The practice of dedicating a child to sanctuary service was known in the ancient Near East; Mesopotamian temples accepted child oblates, children given by families to serve the deity. Hannah's vow draws on this wider cultural form but gives it a specifically Israelite shape: the child belongs to Yahweh, not to a temple institution as such. The location at the doorpost of the sanctuary is the threshold where the sacred and the mundane meet — an appropriate place for Hannah's liminal prayer. Silent prayer was apparently unusual enough that Eli mistook it; most prayer in the ancient world was vocal, even when private. Hannah's interior prayer anticipates a more developed spirituality of individual interiority.
9.literary_structure: The section moves in three beats: Hannah's vow (1:9–11), the exchange with Eli (1:12–17), and the resolution (1:18). The exchange with Eli is structured as misunderstanding-correction-blessing — a common biblical dialogue pattern. The blessing Eli offers functions as prophetic commissioning even though Eli does not know what he is commissioning. The shift in verse 18 — "her face was no longer sad" — is the emotional hinge of the entire chapter; everything before it builds toward this moment, and everything after expands from it.
9.theological_application: Calvin, commenting on Hannah's prayer, observes that true prayer is distinguished from magical petition by the spirit in which the gift is received: Hannah holds the desired child loosely enough to offer him back before she has him. This is the posture the New Testament calls "not my will but yours" — bringing desire fully into the presence of God while releasing control of the outcome. The transformation of Hannah's countenance before any material change is a pattern that runs through the Psalms: the lament psalm often ends in praise not because circumstances have changed but because the act of bringing grief before God has reoriented the soul. Eli's misjudgment cautions against reading outward form as the measure of spiritual reality; the woman who prays silently at the doorpost may be deeper in encounter with God than the priest who watches from his seat. For those who pray long for things long withheld, Hannah's experience offers the possibility that the prayer itself may be transformative even when the answer is deferred.
9.christology: Hannah's vow — asking for a son and immediately consecrating him to God — anticipates the pattern of the Father giving the Son for the world's redemption. The son given by divine gift is simultaneously the son offered back; the logic of gift and dedication runs from Hannah's vow through Abraham's binding of Isaac (Gen 22) to the incarnation itself, where the Father sends the Son in the fullness of time (Gal 4:4). The Nazirite consecration — separation, purity, lifelong service — prefigures the one who was holy from the womb (Luke 1:35) and who came not to do his own will but the will of the one who sent him (John 6:38). Eli's blessing, spoken in ignorance, reflects the pattern of priestly intercession that finds its fulfillment in Christ as our great High Priest who always lives to intercede (Heb 7:25).

19.synthesis:
The Lord remembers Hannah — the same verb used of God remembering Rachel (Gen 30:22) and remembering his covenant with Israel in Egypt (Exod 2:24). Divine remembrance is not the retrieval of forgotten information but the decisive movement of God toward the one he has covenanted to care for. Hannah conceives, bears a son, and names him Samuel — the name she glosses as "I have asked him from the Lord." The etymology is contested (the name may derive from shem-El, "name of God," or shama, "heard"), but Hannah's interpretation prevails: the child is the embodiment of answered prayer. When the next annual journey to Shiloh comes, Hannah stays home to nurse the child. She will bring him when he is weaned, and he will remain at Shiloh forever. Elkanah acquiesces: "Do what seems best to you." There is a quiet dignity in Hannah's authority here — she received the promise in prayer, she will fulfill the vow in her own time.

The weaning scene is charged with concentrated devotion. Hannah brings Samuel to Shiloh with a significant sacrificial offering — three bulls, an ephah of flour, a jar of wine — not the minimum required but an extravagant acknowledgment of what she has received. She presents the boy to Eli: this is the child I prayed for, the Lord granted my petition, and I am lending him to the Lord for as long as he lives. The Hebrew word sha'al — asked — plays throughout: Samuel (Shemu'el), asked of God (sha'alti), lent to God (hish'iltihu). The entire episode is woven into the texture of petition, answer, and return.

Keil and Delitzsch observe that the act of dedicating Samuel to Shiloh is not abandonment but the fulfillment of the deepest intention of Hannah's love. She asked for him from God; she now gives him back to God. The child who was the answer to her prayer becomes the means by which Israel will be answered in its need for prophetic leadership. John Gill notes that the sacrifice of three bulls suggests the magnitude of Hannah's gratitude — this is no routine peace offering but a feast of thanksgiving whose extravagance reflects what she believes she has received.
19.historical_context: Weaning in the ancient world typically occurred at two to three years of age, significantly later than modern Western norms. The period of nursing was thus a relatively extended one, giving Hannah several years with Samuel before his dedication at Shiloh. The sacrificial package Hannah brings — three bulls, flour, and wine — corresponds to the proper accompaniments for major burnt offerings as specified in Num 15:8–10. Child oblates (children dedicated to temple or sanctuary service) were a known institution in the ancient Near East; Samuel's dedication follows this cultural form but transforms its meaning through the covenant framework of Israel's worship. Shiloh's role as the central sanctuary would last until the ark's capture (1 Sam 4:11) and Shiloh's subsequent destruction (referenced in Jer 7:12; Ps 78:60). Samuel's establishment there as a child sets him in the institution that will shortly collapse, through which collapse he will emerge as the transitional figure carrying prophetic authority independent of any fixed sanctuary.
19.literary_structure: The section divides neatly: conception and birth (1:19–20), Hannah's decision to stay home (1:21–23), and the presentation at Shiloh (1:24–28). The wordplay on sha'al (ask/lend) binds the three movements together. The section closes with the entire household worshiping — a corporate response to Hannah's private petition, demonstrating that her faithfulness has drawn others into the orbit of praise.
19.theological_application: Matthew Henry draws from Hannah's dedication a principle of consecrated parenthood: children given by God are given in trust, not in ownership. Hannah's posture — "I have lent him to the Lord" — is the appropriate Christian frame for parenting, in which parents receive children as gifts held in stewardship rather than acquisitions to be possessed. The discipline of fulfilling a costly vow when the fulfillment is actually painful (giving up a long-desired child is harder than promising to) tests the integrity of devotion. Hannah does not reduce the vow; she brings Samuel when he is weaned, as promised, and she brings an extravagant offering as well. The Puritan divines read this as a pattern for how Christians should dedicate their children to the Lord in baptism — not as a magical guarantee of salvation but as a public commitment to raise them in the knowledge and service of God, returning to the Lord what the Lord has given.
19.christology: Samuel presented at Shiloh and left there to serve the Lord all his life anticipates the presentation of the child Jesus at the temple (Luke 2:22–38), where Simeon recognizes him as the one who fulfills Israel's hopes. As Samuel is dedicated to serve at the central sanctuary that will eventually be superseded, so the child Jesus is presented at the Jerusalem temple that he himself will supersede by becoming the true temple, the meeting point between God and humanity (John 2:19–21). The motif of the miraculous child consecrated from birth to divine service — Samuel, Samson, John the Baptist, Jesus — forms a crescendo in which each successive figure points beyond himself to the one who is consecrated not by vow but by nature, not for a season but eternally. Hannah's song in chapter 2 will make this trajectory explicit by speaking of the Lord's anointed king long before any king exists in Israel.
